name: Password Strength Checker
scenario: Every signup form on the internet has one — the password strength indicator. As users type, the meter shifts from 'Weak' to 'Strong' based on what they've typed. You're building this engine for a security-conscious fintech app (think Razorpay, Stripe, HDFC NetBanking). Real password strength requires checking multiple criteria: length, character diversity (uppercase, lowercase, digits, special chars), common-password detection, and absence of obvious patterns. This is real security code — sloppy implementation here has led to actual breaches at real companies.
description: Build a `PasswordChecker` class that evaluates passwords against multiple criteria. Each criterion is a separate method. The final score determines the strength label. Practice character-level analysis, multiple checks composed together, and a touch of real-world security awareness.

core.description: `hasUppercase(String password)` — true if contains at least one uppercase letter (A-Z)
`hasLowercase(String password)` — true if contains at least one lowercase letter (a-z)
`hasDigit(String password)` — true if contains at least one digit (0-9)
`hasSpecialChar(String password)` — true if contains at least one of: !@#$%^&*()
`isCommonPassword(String password)` — returns true if password (case-insensitive) matches any of: 'password', '12345678', 'qwerty', 'admin'
`getStrength(String password)` — returns 'Weak', 'Medium', or 'Strong' based on number of criteria met
main() tests with at least 5 different password examples (showing weak, medium, strong)

bonus.description: Add `hasNoCommonPattern(String password)` — returns false if password contains '1234' or 'abcd' (case-insensitive)
Print a CHECKLIST showing which criteria passed/failed (✓ or ✗)
Calculate a numeric score (0-100) based on criteria met, weighted by importance
Add `suggestImprovement(String password)` — returns a string telling the user what to add

data_to_use:
Test with these passwords:

1. `"abc"` — weak (short, missing everything)
2. `"password"` — weak (common, missing uppercase, digits, special)
3. `"Password1"` — medium (length OK, has 3 criteria)
4. `"P@ssw0rd123"` — strong (all 5 criteria)
5. `"12345678"` — weak (common password)

Expected scoring:
- 0-2 criteria: Weak
- 3-4 criteria: Medium
- 5 criteria: Strong

Common passwords automatically score 'Weak' regardless of other criteria.
example_output:
===================================
      PASSWORD STRENGTH CHECK
===================================
'abc' → Weak
'password' → Weak (common password!)
'Password1' → Medium
'P@ssw0rd123' → Strong
'12345678' → Weak (common password!)
===================================

criteria: hasUppercase iterates through chars correctly (10 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 AI hints. You have all the tools (charAt, contains, equalsIgnoreCase, iteration). The challenge is composing many simple methods into the getStrength logic. Plan: write each check method, then getStrength counts how many returned true.
submission_instructions: Save as PasswordChecker.java. Run it. Try edge cases: empty string, single character, very long password. Push to GitHub portfolio — this is real security utility code.
tips_for_success: For character checks: use Character.isUpperCase(c), Character.isLowerCase(c), Character.isDigit(c). These are cleaner than manual ASCII comparison.
For hasSpecialChar: check if a string like "!@#$%^&*()" contains the character. Use .indexOf(c) != -1 or build a Set of allowed chars.
For isCommonPassword: use String[] commonPasswords array, loop through, equalsIgnoreCase each.
For getStrength: count how many criteria methods returned true. Use the count to determine level.
Special case: if isCommonPassword returns true, return Weak IMMEDIATELY regardless of other criteria.
For the bonus checklist: print each criterion's pass/fail using ✓ and ✗ (or true/false if encoding is an issue).
concepts_reinforced: Method composition (each check is a method, getStrength uses them all)
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this: Beyond the syntax, you experience HOW REAL SECURITY VALIDATION IS BUILT. Real password validators (in OWASP libraries, in NIST guidelines, in Spring Security) do exactly this — composing many small criteria checks. The actual rules are more complex (entropy calculation, breach database lookups), but the structure is identical. Notice: each check is INDEPENDENT and TESTABLE. That's the key — small, focused, composable methods. This is the engineering style you'll use for years.
extensions_for_advanced_learners: Add an entropy calculator (estimate bits of randomness)
Check for personal info (user's name shouldn't be in password)
Add policy customization (configurable min length, required criteria)
Hash the password with SHA-256 before storage (preview of real auth)
